filter_index.video_title: Chandrakhani Pass Trek | Himachal's Most Loved Pass Climb Visual Video | By Ninad Lanjewar

snow_or_rain_notes: June: high snow depth from Chaklani onward, on both sides of the pass
July: snow melting rapidly, good patches remain on higher reaches of the pass
August: most snow gone, except some patches near Chandrakhani Pass
September: no snow on the trail
Monsoon showers begin mid-July and last until mid-September
temperature_notes: June: daytime 15–20°C, nighttime 1–5°C; 3 warm layers required
July and August: daytime 15–20°C, nighttime 1–5°C; 3 warm layers required
September: daytime 12–15°C, nighttime 0–3°C; 4 warm layers required
Early October: nippy air, early winter chill, night temperatures below zero at most camps, icy cold at higher camps

terrain_challenges: Uneven trails with daily ascents and descents
Long ledge walk leading to the pass requiring careful navigation
Relentless forest trail climb from Rumsu to meadows (9 km, 3500+ ft gain)
Slushy/mushy forest trail ahead of Rumsu after rain
Snow-covered paths requiring fresh tracks to be cut
altitude_or_weather_risks: Trek reaches 12,250 ft (12,185 ft per elevation gain) above sea level
Day 1 rapid gain of 3500+ ft from Rumsu to Naya Tapru increases AMS susceptibility
Above 10,000 ft AMS risk is real for both first-time and experienced trekkers
Even fit trekkers climbing too high too soon are at equal AMS risk
HAPE and HACE are referenced as altitude-related risks

fitness_benchmark: You must be able to walk, jog or run 5 km in under 40 minutes. This is the minimum recommended fitness level that you must aim for.
approval_requirements: Make sure you upload these screenshots 15 to 20 days before your trek date. We want to see that you are being consistent in your preparation.
Your screenshots must demonstrate that you are meeting the fitness criteria for your trek.
Your runs don't meet your trek's fitness criteria at least 3 times in the past month.

experience_themes: June: snow patches and snow-bound sections near Chaklani and above
July: snow melting rapidly, first monsoon showers, wildflowers sprouting in the green belt on the Kullu side, trekkers wade through beds of wildflowers
Mid-July to end of August: vibrant green grasslands, slopes alive with swaying wildflowers, cloudy skies, good showers
Post-mid-September: skies clear, early autumn colours on slopes, most colourful pass, gorgeous sunrises and sunsets, snow-clad mountains against deep blue skies
Monsoon: lush forests, full streams, trails come alive
Mid-July to end of August: vibrant green grasslands, slopes alive with swaying wildflowers
primary_watchouts: High snow depth in June makes pass-crossing challenging
Monsoon brings landslides, roadblocks, traffic jams and delays en route to base camp
Early October: streams dry up, water sources become increasingly difficult
Indiahikes may reroute, reschedule, or cancel treks in unforeseen monsoon situations
Trekkers averse to monsoon showers and cloudy skies
Trekkers needing reliable water sources (early October)
best_for: Trekkers seeking wildflowers and lush green landscapes (mid-July to end of August)
Trekkers seeking clear skies, autumn colours, and mountain views (post-mid-September)
Trekkers comfortable with monsoon showers and cloudy skies
not_ideal_for: Trekkers averse to monsoon showers and cloudy skies
Trekkers needing reliable water sources (early October)
Trekkers uncomfortable with high snow depth on the pass (June)
Trekkers who cannot handle rapid elevation gain and AMS risk
Trekkers not prepared for uneven ascents/descents and endurance-testing climbs
